question: What is the recommended inspection interval for the HPC?
answer: The recommended inspection interval for the HPC is every 30 operational cycles under nominal conditions, reduced to every 10 cycles once any HPC parameter crosses its alarm threshold.

plan.rationale: The question is about the recommended inspection interval for the HPC, which can be answered from manuals.

sources_text:
[doc_id=manual-hpc | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[High-Pressure Compressor (HPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
Borescope inspection of the HPC stages is recommended every **30 operational cycles**
under nominal conditions, reduced to every **10 cycles** once any HPC parameter
crosses its alarm threshold.

[doc_id=manual-hpt | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[High-Pressure Turbine (HPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
HPT clearance and seal inspection every **30 operational cycles**, aligned with the
HPC schedule since the two are mechanically coupled on the high-pressure shaft.

[doc_id=manual-fan | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[Fan Module Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
Fan blade and containment inspection every **50 operational cycles**. Because the fan
is not the primary fault mode in this fleet, routine intervals are longer than for the
HPC.

[doc_id=manual-lpc | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[Low-Pressure Compressor (LPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
Combined gas-path inspection every **40 operational cycles**; the LPC is typically
inspected alongside the HPC because the two share the core gas path.

[doc_id=fault-FC-HPC-001 | section: Procedure]
[FC-HPC-001 — High-Pressure Compressor Degradation — Procedure]
1. Confirm the primary signature on at least the last 5 operational cycles (suppress
   single-cycle noise spikes).
2. Reduce HPC borescope inspection interval to every 10 cycles.
3. Perform a compressor wash; re-baseline T30 and Ps30 after the wash.
4. If T30 and Ps30 remain in alarm after wash, schedule HPC module removal at the next
   maintenance opportunity and begin RUL tracking for end-of-life planning.
5. Check whether T50 (LPT) has also entered alarm — co-occurrence indicates
   degradation has propagated and shortens the remaining-useful-life estimate.

question: What is the recommended procedure once FC-HPC-001 is confirmed?
answer: Once FC-HPC-001 is confirmed, the recommended procedure is to: 1) Confirm the primary signature on at least the last 5 operational cycles. 2) Reduce HPC borescope inspection interval to every 10 cycles. 3) Perform a compressor wash and re-baseline T30 and Ps30 after the wash. 4) If T30 and Ps30 remain in alarm after the wash, schedule HPC module removal at the next maintenance opportunity and begin RUL tracking for end-of-life planning. 5) Check whether T50 (LPT) has also entered alarm, as co-occurrence indicates degradation has propagated and shortens the remaining-useful-life estimate.

plan.rationale: The question is about a procedure related to a fault code, which can be answered from manuals or work orders.

sources_text:
[doc_id=fault-code-reference | section: Usage notes]
[Fault Code Reference Index — Usage notes]
- **HPC degradation (FC-HPC-001) is the dominant fault mode in this fleet.** When in
  doubt, evaluate the HPC signature first.
- A single parameter in alarm is an *advisory*, not a fault. Raise a coded fault only
  when the full primary signature is present, to suppress sensor-noise false alarms.
- Codes are not mutually exclusive: late-life engines frequently show FC-HPC-001 and
  FC-LPT-001 together as degradation propagates downstream.

[doc_id=fault-FC-LPT-001 | section: Procedure]
[FC-LPT-001 — Low-Pressure Turbine Thermal Degradation — Procedure]
1. Confirm sustained T50 rise over at least 5 cycles, with W32 trending down.
2. Inspect LPT blades and exhaust path for distress and blockage.
3. Inspect the LPT coolant circuit; if W31 (HPT coolant, sensor 20) is also low,
   treat as a shared cooling-supply fault rather than an isolated LPT problem.
4. When FC-LPT-001 co-occurs with FC-HPC-001, prioritize end-of-life planning — the
   combination is a strong late-life indicator.

[doc_id=wo-1007-engine58 | section: Action taken]
[WO-1007 — Engine 58 rising bleed enthalpy — Action taken]
Raised FC-HPC-001. Compressor wash performed; began close RUL tracking given the
combined late-life signature. Recommended end-of-life planning review.

[doc_id=manual-hpc | section: 4. Alarm response]
[High-Pressure Compressor (HPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T30 ≥ 1607.89 degR:** HPC discharge over-temperature. Inspect for blade fouling,
  tip-clearance loss, and seal wear. Correlate with Ps30 and phi.
- **Ps30 ≥ 48.10 psia** with **P30 ≤ 551.16 psia:** classic HPC efficiency loss
  signature — the stage is generating more static rise but less recoverable total
  pressure. Schedule wash and clearance check.
- **phi ≤ 519.69 pps/psi:** fuel-flow-to-pressure ratio falling; cross-check fuel
  metering and confirm against T30 trend before acting.

When two or more HPC parameters are simultaneously in alarm, raise f

[doc_id=fault-FC-HPC-001 | section: Procedure]
[FC-HPC-001 — High-Pressure Compressor Degradation — Procedure]
1. Confirm the primary signature on at least the last 5 operational cycles (suppress
   single-cycle noise spikes).
2. Reduce HPC borescope inspection interval to every 10 cycles.
3. Perform a compressor wash; re-baseline T30 and Ps30 after the wash.
4. If T30 and Ps30 remain in alarm after wash, schedule HPC module removal at the next
   maintenance opportunity and begin RUL tracking for end-of-life planning.
5. Check whether T50 (LPT) has also entered alarm — co-occurrence indicates
   degradation has propagated and shortens the remaining-useful-life estimate.

question: What does a rising bypass ratio (BPR) with no HPC alarm indicate?
answer: A rising bypass ratio (BPR) with no HPC alarm usually indicates a secondary effect rather than a fan fault, often related to core or HPC degradation.

plan.rationale: The question is conceptual about the meaning of a rising bypass ratio (BPR) in the context of the fleet's sensors and subsystems, which can be answered from manuals.

sources_text:
[doc_id=wo-1006-engine33 | section: Observation]
[WO-1006 — Engine 33 bypass-ratio drift — Observation]
Engine 33's bypass ratio (BPR) drifted to 8.55, just over the **8.54** alarm threshold
and above the **8.48** nominal maximum. Per the fan manual, rising BPR with no
corresponding HPC alarm is usually a secondary effect rather than a fan fault.

[doc_id=manual-fan | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Fan Module Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **BPR ≥ 8.54:** rising bypass ratio indicates the core is passing less of the
  total flow — often a secondary symptom of core/HPC degradation rather than a fan
  fault. Correlate with HPC parameters before attributing to the fan.
- **Nf ≥ 2388.30 rpm / NRf ≥ 2388.28 rpm:** physical and corrected fan speed creeping
  above nominal. Confirm against demanded fan speed; a gap suggests control-loop drift.

[doc_id=wo-1006-engine33 | section: Status]
[WO-1006 — Engine 33 bypass-ratio drift — Status]
Open (advisory) — monitor; escalate only if HPC parameters subsequently enter alarm.

[doc_id=wo-1006-engine33 | section: Action taken]
[WO-1006 — Engine 33 bypass-ratio drift — Action taken]
Reviewed HPC parameters — all nominal, so this was logged as an isolated fan advisory,
not FC-HPC-001. No hardware action; flagged for re-review in 25 cycles.

[doc_id=wo-1007-engine58 | section: Observation]
[WO-1007 — Engine 58 rising bleed enthalpy — Observation]
Engine 58's bleed enthalpy (htBleed) reached 398.1, above the **397.50** alarm
threshold, accompanied by HPC discharge temperature (T30) at 1608.7 degR, over its
**1607.89 degR** threshold. Rising bleed enthalpy plus rising T30 is a strong late-life
HPC-degradation indicator.

question: Give me an overview of engine 76's current status.
answer: Engine 76 has completed 210 operational cycles. Currently, 5 sensors are in alarm: T50, P30, Ps30, phi, and NRf. The T50 sensor is in alarm with a value of 1432.07, exceeding its threshold of 1428.11. The P30 sensor is slightly below its threshold with a value of 550.94 against a threshold of 551.16. The Ps30 sensor is in alarm with a value of 48.24, exceeding its threshold of 48.1. The phi sensor is slightly below its threshold with a value of 519.41 against a threshold of 519.69. The NRf sensor is exactly at its threshold with a value of 2388.28.

plan.rationale: The question asks for a whole-engine summary, which includes the current status and operational cycles of engine 76.

question: Engine 47 is showing elevated Ps30 readings — is this a known fault pattern, and what does the manual say to do?
route_blurb: Answered from documents and sensor telemetry together.
answer: Engine 47 is showing a known fault pattern of HPC degradation. The elevated Ps30 reading is part of the primary signature for fault code FC-HPC-001, which indicates HPC efficiency loss. The manual advises scheduling a wash and clearance check when Ps30 is ≥ 48.10 psia and P30 is ≤ 551.16 psia, which matches the current conditions of Engine 47.

contexts.section: Primary signature (all should be present to raise the code)

plan.rationale: The question asks about the status of the Ps30 reading and whether it is a known fault, requiring both live data and manual reference.

sources_text:
[doc_id=fault-FC-HPC-001 | section: Primary signature (all should be present to raise the code)]
[FC-HPC-001 — High-Pressure Compressor Degradation — Primary signature (all should be present to raise the code)]
| Sensor | Symbol | Direction | Alarm condition |
|--------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| 3  | T30  | rising  | ≥ 1607.89 degR |
| 11 | Ps30 | rising  | ≥ 48.10 psia   |
| 7  | P30  | falling | ≤ 551.16 psia  |
| 12 | phi  | falling | ≤ 519.69 pps/psi |

[doc_id=wo-1002-engine47 | section: Observation]
[WO-1002 — Engine 47 HPC pressure-loss investigation — Observation]
Engine 47 showed HPC total discharge pressure (P30) falling to 550.8 psia, below the
**551.16 psia** low alarm threshold, with the fuel-flow ratio (phi) at 519.4 pps/psi,
under its **519.69** low threshold. T30 was elevated but not yet in alarm. This is the
"pressure-loss-first" presentation of HPC degradation.

[doc_id=fault-code-reference | section: Usage notes]
[Fault Code Reference Index — Usage notes]
- **HPC degradation (FC-HPC-001) is the dominant fault mode in this fleet.** When in
  doubt, evaluate the HPC signature first.
- A single parameter in alarm is an *advisory*, not a fault. Raise a coded fault only
  when the full primary signature is present, to suppress sensor-noise false alarms.
- Codes are not mutually exclusive: late-life engines frequently show FC-HPC-001 and
  FC-LPT-001 together as degradation propagates downstream.

[doc_id=manual-hpc | section: 4. Alarm response]
[High-Pressure Compressor (HPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T30 ≥ 1607.89 degR:** HPC discharge over-temperature. Inspect for blade fouling,
  tip-clearance loss, and seal wear. Correlate with Ps30 and phi.
- **Ps30 ≥ 48.10 psia** with **P30 ≤ 551.16 psia:** classic HPC efficiency loss
  signature — the stage is generating more static rise but less recoverable total
  pressure. Schedule wash and clearance check.
- **phi ≤ 519.69 pps/psi:** fuel-flow-to-pressure ratio falling; cross-check fuel
  metering and confirm against T30 trend before acting.

When two or more HPC parameters are simultaneously in alarm, raise f

question: Engine 24's HPC discharge temperature T30 is high — what's the fault and the procedure?
route_blurb: Answered from documents and sensor telemetry together.
answer: The fault for Engine 24 is HPC degradation, indicated by the fault code FC-HPC-001. The procedure involves inspecting for blade fouling, tip-clearance loss, and seal wear, and correlating with Ps30 and phi. Since multiple parameters are in alarm, the procedure also includes scheduling a wash and clearance check, and re-baselining T30/Ps30 after the wash. If the parameters are still in alarm, escalate to module removal.

plan.rationale: The question asks for both the fault related to the high HPC discharge temperature (T30) and the procedure, which requires both live sensor data and manual information.

question: Engine 91's LPT outlet temperature is elevated — what fault is this and what should we do?
route_blurb: Answered from documents and sensor telemetry together.
answer: The elevated LPT outlet temperature in Engine 91 is identified as an LPT over-temperature fault. The fault code associated with this condition is FC-LPT-001. The recommended action is to inspect for blade distress and exhaust-path blockage, as persistent high T50 is a leading indicator of end-of-life in engines with downstream degradation. Additionally, the low LPT coolant bleed (W32) suggests inspecting the cooling circuit and cross-checking for a shared supply fault.

plan.rationale: The question asks about the elevated LPT outlet temperature, which requires both the current status of the sensor and the corresponding manual procedures for addressing the fault.

sources_text:
[doc_id=manual-lpt | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Low-Pressure Turbine (LPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T50 ≥ 1428.11 degR:** LPT over-temperature. Inspect for blade distress and
  exhaust-path blockage. Persistent high T50 is a leading indicator of end-of-life in
  engines whose primary degradation has propagated downstream.
- **W32 ≤ 23.00 lbm/s:** falling LPT coolant bleed; inspect the cooling circuit and
  cross-check W31 (sensor 20) for a shared supply fault. See fault code FC-LPT-001.

[doc_id=wo-1009-engine91 | section: overview]
[WO-1009 — Engine 91 combined LPT thermal + coolant event — overview]
**Engine:** 91 **Date:** 2026-06-08 **Raised by:** prognostics review
**Fault code:** FC-LPT-001

[doc_id=wo-1009-engine91 | section: Observation]
[WO-1009 — Engine 91 combined LPT thermal + coolant event — Observation]
Engine 91 presented the full FC-LPT-001 signature: LPT outlet temperature (T50) at
1431.2 degR, above the **1428.11 degR** threshold, with LPT coolant bleed (W32) at
22.9 lbm/s, below the **23.00 lbm/s** low threshold. HPC parameters were also
elevated, indicating degradation had propagated downstream from the core gas path.

[doc_id=wo-1003-engine76 | section: Observation]
[WO-1003 — Engine 76 LPT exhaust over-temperature — Observation]
Engine 76's LPT outlet temperature (T50) reached 1430.6 degR, above the **1428.11
degR** alarm threshold, sustained over 7 cycles. Coolant bleed (W32) was trending
toward its low threshold. HPC parameters were nominal, so this was treated as a
primary LPT event rather than propagated HPC degradation.

[doc_id=manual-lpt | section: 2. Monitored parameters]

| Sensor | Symbol | Quantity | Nominal range | Alarm |
|--------|--------|----------|---------------|-------|
| 4  | T50 | LPT outlet total temperature (degR) | 1390.08 – 1415.43 | ≥ 1428.11 (high) |
| 21 | W32 | LPT coolant bleed (lbm/s)           | 23.18 – 23.53     | ≤ 23.00 (low)    |
